Lab Guide › Research Workflow

Use AI

The lab's rules and guidelines for using generative AI responsibly in research.

Getting Started

  • Start guide: Undergraduates
  • Start guide: Graduate students
  • Start guide: Postdocs

  • Work expectations
  • Time off & vacation
  • Support resources
  • Be safe
  • Pay for things
  • Travel
  • Ship packages
  • Lab operations roles

Research Workflow

  • Develop a research hypothesis
  • Review the literature
  • Develop quarterly SMART goals
  • Manage data
  • Code
  • Analyze data
  • Analyze error & uncertainty
  • Use AI

Writing & Dissemination

  • Write a first draft
  • Design your figures
  • Paper revisions
  • Review papers
  • Make a poster
  • Present
  • Brand & communication

Communication & Culture

  • Communicating in the lab
  • Discussion guidelines
  • Meetings
  • Journal club

Professional Development

  • Attending a conference
  • Applying for funding
  • Request recommendation letters
  • File your dissertation

Large language models are changing how we do science. We move with the times while developing as scientists and engineers and holding to high, reproducible standards. As a lab, we agree to the following.

Protect sensitive information. Do not use generative-AI tools with sensitive UC Davis systems or data. Never provide inputs that might contain student information, personnel data, patient data, confidential financial or research information, or otherwise sensitive university information.

Appropriate uses

  • Code debugging and coding assistance (e.g., Copilot).
  • Improving the code that generates R or Python plots.
  • Editing existing drafts for grammar and sentence structure.
  • Brainstorming ideas — as long as you don’t limit brainstorming to AI alone.

Inappropriate uses

  • Initial writing drafts.
  • Believing what AI tells you without double-checking and doing your due diligence.
  • Generating images for publication.
  • Uploading any data produced or collected in our lab or others’.
  • Fabricating or falsifying data.
  • Sharing any personnel or student information.
  • Presenting AI content as your own — all use must be acknowledged, and AI is never an author.
  • Avoiding learning or problem-solving in favor of trusting AI; that defeats the purpose of research.

AI golden rule: use AI with others as you would want them to use AI with you. — Stanford Report

When you do use AI

Acknowledge it. If a generative tool is used, your publications should acknowledge which tool was used and how — even if no AI-generated content was incorporated.

Be aware of limitations. AI can reflect bias and stereotypes, produce fake citations, and perform calculations incorrectly. Always verify against tools designed for the task.

Follow the UC Davis Code of Academic Conduct. Review and verify any AI-generated content you publish, and consider copyright — you are responsible for anything you publish. Never copy-paste AI language or use AI to generate images for publications.

References: UC Davis & Generative AI guidance · UC Davis student guide to GenAI
